lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: Last night, I hear, they lay at Northampton;

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: At Stony-Stratford will they be to-night:

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: To-morrow, or next day, they will be here.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I long with all my heart to see the prince:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But I hear, no; they say my son of York

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Hath almost overta’en him in his growth.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Ay, mother; but I would not have it so.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Why, my young cousin, it is good to grow.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Grandam, one night, as we did sit at supper,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: My uncle Rivers talk’d how I did grow

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: More than my brother: “Ay,” quoth my uncle Gloucester,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: “Small herbs have grace, great weeds do grow apace:”

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: And since, methinks, I would not grow so fast,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: In him that did object the same to thee:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: He was the wretched’st thing when he was young,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: So long a-growing and so leisurely,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: That, if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: Why, madam, so, no doubt, he is.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I hope he is; but yet let mothers doubt.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Now, by my troth, if I had been remember’d,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I could have given my uncle’s grace a flout,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: To touch his growth nearer than he touch’d mine.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: How, my pretty York? I pray thee, let me hear it.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Marry, they say my uncle grew so fast

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old:

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: ’Twas full two years ere I could get a tooth.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I pray thee, pretty York, who told thee this?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Grandam, his nurse.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: His nurse! why, she was dead ere thou wert born.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: If ’twere not she, I cannot tell who told me.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: A parlous boy: go to, you are too shrewd.

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: Good madam, be not angry with the child.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Pitchers have ears.

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: Here comes a messenger. What news?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Such news, my lord, as grieves me to unfold.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: How fares the prince?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Well, madam, and in health.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: What is thy news then?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: With them Sir Thomas Vaughan, prisoners.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Who hath committed them?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The mighty dukes

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Gloucester and Buckingham.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: For what offence?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The sum of all I can, I have disclosed;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Why or for what these nobles were committed

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Is all unknown to me, my gracious lady.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Ay me, I see the downfall of our house!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Insulting tyranny begins to jet

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Upon the innocent and aweless throne:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Welcome, destruction, death, and massacre!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I see, as in a map, the end of all.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Accursed and unquiet wrangling days,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: How many of you have mine eyes beheld!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: My husband lost his life to get the crown;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And often up and down my sons were toss’d,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: For me to joy and weep their gain and loss:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And being seated, and domestic broils

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Clean over-blown, themselves, the conquerors,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Make war upon themselves; blood against blood,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Self against self: O, preposterous

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And frantic outrage, end thy damned spleen;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Or let me die, to look on death no more!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Come, come, my boy; we will to sanctuary.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Madam, farewell.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I’ll go along with you.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: You have no cause.

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: My gracious lady, go;

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: And thither bear your treasure and your goods.

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: For my part, I’ll resign unto your grace

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: The seal I keep: and so betide to me

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: As well I tender you and all of yours!

lines.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
lines.text: Come, I’ll conduct you to the sanctuary.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Archbishop of York, the young Duke of York, Queen Elizabeth, and the Duchess of York.

glosses.definition: town in Northamptonshire — stopping point on the route from Ludlow
Stony Stratford — the nearby village where Richard in fact arrested Rivers and Grey
caught up with him in height

glosses.phrase: Small herbs have grace, great weeds do grow apace
glosses.definition: small plants are graceful; weeds grow fast — Gloucester's proverb, turned against him by the clever York
if I had recollected (the story)
mocking reply; jeer

glosses.phrase: gnaw a crust at two hours old
glosses.definition: the folk-tale of Richard born with teeth — transmitted by More and Holinshed, Shakespeare's source
sharp joke — York's deliberate pun on 'biting' given the teeth legend
dangerously precocious; perilous (from 'perilous')
jugs have handles ('ears') and children overhear adult talk — proverbial
Pontefract Castle, Yorkshire — notorious royal prison; site of Rivers, Grey, Vaughan's execution

glosses.phrase: tiger now hath seized the gentle hind
glosses.definition: tiger seizing the doe — emblem of tyranny preying on innocence
strut, swagger
throne that commands no awe — a child-king's weak majesty
as if in a map, in miniature — Elizabeth sees the whole catastrophe laid out
quarrelsome, strife-filled times — the whole Wars of the Roses in one phrase
civil strife; quarrels at home
reversed, upside-down (Latin pre-post — before-after inverted)
passion, ill-temper — the spleen was believed the seat of violent emotions
we will take refuge in Westminster Abbey's sanctuary — church ground immune to arrest
the Great Seal of England — the Archbishop's office to keep; he surrenders it to Elizabeth
cherish, care for

annotations.title: Stony Stratford and the arrest of Rivers and Grey
annotations.body: The messenger's news — that Rivers, Grey, and Vaughan have been sent to Pomfret — compresses the historical crisis of late April 1483. The young prince, escorted by Rivers and Grey, was met at Stony Stratford in Northamptonshire by Richard and Buckingham, travelling with a larger force. Richard arrested Rivers at nearby Northampton on 30 April and seized the prince at Stony Stratford on 1 May; Rivers, Grey, and Vaughan were sent north to Pomfret where they were executed in June. The play compresses these events offstage, but the dates, places, and names are accurate to Holinshed's chronicle. What the play adds is the women's immediate recognition of what has happened: Elizabeth's "I see the downfall of our house" and her decision to take sanctuary at Westminster Abbey. Shakespeare's stage condenses historical weeks into minutes, but the political grammar survives the compression.

annotations.title: York's tongue: precocity read and feared
annotations.body: The young Duke of York's quick-witted exchange with his grandmother — the nurse who supposedly said Richard was born with teeth and therefore "grew so fast / That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old" — is one of the play's showcase moments of child-speech. York's rhetorical quickness is praised onstage (Buckingham: "With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!") and feared behind the hand. Richard's own judgement (3.1.151 — "a parlous boy") will seal the child's fate. Shakespeare does something difficult here: he lets the boy be funny and then shows us that his very liveness is what dooms him. Tudor policy around royal children required their formative concealment; visible intelligence in a boy-heir was a political liability. The play lets us feel the tightening of a net we cannot prevent.
sources: Ann Blake, "Children and Suffering in Shakespeare's Plays" (1998)
Carol Chillington Rutter, Shakespeare and Child's Play (2007)

annotations.title: "The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind"
annotations.body: Elizabeth's image for Richard's arrest of her brother and son — "The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind" — introduces the play's predator-prey register that will intensify through Margaret's 4.4 imagery (wolves and lambs, hell-hound and butcher'd princes). What distinguishes this image is its political specificity: Elizabeth is not speaking of generic predation but of "insulting tyranny" jetting upon "the innocent and aweless throne." The throne is "aweless" because its occupant is a child: the political body has lost its commanding presence. Elizabeth's phrase "I see, as in a map, the end of all" is one of the play's strongest compressed images of political foresight — she sees the whole sequence at once. What she cannot do, yet, is prevent it.

trials.prompt: The arrest of Rivers and Grey took place historically where, according to the chronicle sources Shakespeare used?
options: At Ludlow Castle, before the prince's departure
At Northampton and Stony Stratford in late April 1483
At the Tower of London after Edward IV's death
At Dover, as they attempted to leave for Burgundy

trials.prompt: The young Duke of York's joke about Richard's birth-teeth (28–30) achieves what dramatic effect?
options: It introduces pure comic relief, unrelated to the plot
It is clever enough to be praised aloud and feared silently — the child's visible intelligence is precisely what makes him politically dangerous
It is an unwitting insult the boy does not understand
It is a learned riddle requiring classical knowledge to decode

trials.prompt: Where does Queen Elizabeth take refuge at the scene's end?
options: At her brother Rivers' estate in Northamptonshire

trials.prompt: Elizabeth's "The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind" and "I see, as in a map, the end of all" together characterize her as what?
options: A character given to inexact rhetorical flourish
A political seer whose predator-prey vocabulary and compressed foresight anticipate the play's larger imagery and moral arithmetic
A passive mother with no political awareness
